mission.title: Install and Configure Kusionstack on Kubernetes
mission.description: KusionStack is a declarative intent-driven platform orchestrator designed for internal developer platforms (IDP). Installing KusionStack allows you to manage your application delivery processes more efficiently and effectively.

steps.title: Add the Kusion Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kusion Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kusionstack https://kusionstack.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KusionStack using Helm
steps.description:
Install KusionStack in the 'kusion' namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist.
```bash
helm install kusion kusionstack/kusion --namespace kusion --create-namespace --version v0.15.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the KusionStack pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kusion
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the KusionStack service to access it.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kusion --namespace kusion 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KusionStack will have the Kusion pods running in the 'kusion' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine. Ensure that resource limits are configured as desired.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kusionstack https://kusionstack.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install kusion kusionstack/kusion --namespace kusion --create-namespace --version v0.15.0

kubectl edit deployment kusion --namespace kusion
# Add the following under spec.template.spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the KusionStack release from the 'kusion' namespace using Helm.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims associated with KusionStack.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'kusion' namespace and verify that it has been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Create a backup of the current KusionStack release configuration.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and upgrade the KusionStack release to the latest version.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the KusionStack pods are running successfully after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue may occur if there are configuration errors or resource constraints. To diagnose, check the pod logs using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kusion
```
Fix the underlying issue based on the logs.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the KusionStack service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kusion
```
If the service is down, restart the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the upgrade, check the current version of the release with:
```bash
helm list --namespace kusion
```
Ensure that you are upgrading to a compatible version.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources error
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an insufficient resources error, check the resource limits set in the deployment. You can view the current resource requests and limits with:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kusion --namespace kusion
```
Adjust the resource limits if necessary.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
